findings.title: Ransomware Actors Exploit Unpatched SimpleHelp Remote Monitoring and Management to Compromise Utility Billing Software Provider
findings.summary: Summary The Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) is releasing this advisory in response to ransomware actors leveraging unpatched instances of a vulnerability in SimpleHelp Remote Monitoring and Management (RMM) to compromise customers of a utility billing software provider. This incident reflects a broader pattern of ransomware actors targeting organizations through unpatched versions of SimpleHelp RMM since January 2025. SimpleHelp versions 5.5.7 and earlier contain several vulnerabilities, including CVE-2024-57727 —a path traversal vulnerability. 1 Ransomware actors likely leveraged CVE-2024-57727 to access downstream customers’ unpatched SimpleHelp RMM for disruption of services in double extortion compromises. 1 CISA added CVE-2024-57727 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) Catalog on Feb. 13, 2025. CISA urges software vendors, downstream customers, and end users to immediately implement the Mitigations listed in this advisory based on confirmed compromise or risk of compromise. Download the PDF version of this report: AA25-163A Ransomware Actors Exploit Unpatched SimpleHelp Remote Monitoring and Management to Compromise Utility Billing Software Provider (PDF, 420.49 KB ) Mitigations CISA recommends organizations implement the mitigations below to respond to emerging ransomware activity exploiting SimpleHelp software. These mitigations align with the Cross-Sector Cybersecurity Performance Goals (CPGs) developed by CISA and the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST). The CPGs provide a minimum set of practices and protections that CISA and NIST recommend all organizations implement. CISA and NIST based the CPGs on existing cybersecurity frameworks and guidance to protect against the most common and impactful threats, tactics, techniques, and procedures. Visit CISA’s CPGs webpage for more information on the CPGs, including additional recommended baseline protections. These mitigations apply to all critical infrastructure

enrichment.technical_details: CVE-2024-57727 is a path traversal vulnerability (CWE-22) in SimpleHelp RMM versions 5.5.7 and earlier. Unauthenticated remote attackers can submit crafted HTTP requests containing directory-traversal sequences (e.g., '../') in URL paths or parameters to the SimpleHelp web interface (notably the file/toolbox endpoint) to read arbitrary files from the host. Sensitive files accessible include the server configuration file (configuration/serverconfig.xml) containing hashed admin/user passwords and cryptographic secrets, as well as system files such as /etc/passwd and SSH private keys. The flaw can be chained with CVE-2024-57726 (missing authorization in an admin API) and CVE-2024-57728 (admin-only arbitrary file upload / zip-slip) to escalate from unauthenticated file read to full remote code execution on the SimpleHelp server.
enrichment.affected_products: SimpleHelp Remote Monitoring and Management (RMM) software, version 5.5.7 and all earlier releases (5.5.7 and prior)
enrichment.patch_available: true - https://simple-help.com/blogs/security-vulnerabilities-in-simplehelp-5-5-7-and-earlier-what-you-need-to-know (patches shipped in SimpleHelp versions 5.5.8 through 5.5.10; recommended upgrade to the latest 5.5.x release)
enrichment.active_exploitation: true - CISA Advisory AA25-163A (June 12, 2025) confirms ransomware actors have exploited CVE-2024-57727 against unpatched SimpleHelp RMM instances to compromise a utility billing software provider and downstream customers in double-extortion ransomware attacks since January 2025; CVE-2024-57727 was added to the CISA KEV Catalog on February 13, 2025.
enrichment.threat_actors: DragonForce ransomware actors (per CISA Advisory AA25-163A, June 12, 2025). These actors have been targeting organizations through unpatched SimpleHelp RMM since January 2025.
enrichment.mitigation: Upgrade SimpleHelp server and all deployed Remote Access Service (RAS) endpoints to version 5.5.8 or later (recommended: latest 5.5.x release such as 5.5.15). If immediate patching is not possible: isolate the SimpleHelp server from the internet or stop the SimpleHelp server process; identify and version-check the server by inspecting configuration/serverconfig.xml (or querying https://<server>/allversions); threat-hunt for evidence of compromise (look for executables with three-letter filenames such as aaa.exe, bbb.exe, created after January 2025); monitor inbound/outbound traffic from the SimpleHelp server for anomalies; rotate any credentials/secrets stored in serverconfig.xml; audit downstream customers for indicators of compromise.

findings.title: AI threats in the wild: The current state of prompt injections on the web
findings.summary: Posted by Thomas Brunner, Yu-Han Liu, Moni Pande At Google, our Threat Intelligence teams are dedicated to staying ahead of real-world adversarial activity, proactively monitoring emerging threats before they can impact users. Right now, Indirect Prompt Injection (IPI) is a top priority for the security community, anticipating it as a primary attack vector for adversaries to target and compromise AI agents. But while the danger of IPI is widely discussed, are threat actors actually exploiting this vector today – and if so, how? To answer these questions and to uncover real-world abuse, we initiated a broad sweep of the public web to monitor for known indirect prompt injection patterns. This is what we found. The threat of indirect prompt injection Unlike a direct injection where a user "jailbreaks" a chatbot, IPI occurs when an AI system processes content—like a website, email, or document—that contains malicious instructions. When the AI reads this poisoned content, it may silently follow the attacker's commands instead of the user's original intent. This is not a new area of concern for us and Google has been working tirelessly to combat these threats. Our efforts involve cross-functional collaboration between researchers at Google DeepMind (GDM) and defenders like the Google Threat Intelligence Group (GTIG). We have previously detailed our work in this area and researchers have further highlighted the evolving nature of these vulnerabilities. Despite this collective focus, a fundamental question remains: to what degree are real-world malicious actors currently operationalizing these attacks? Proactive monitoring at Google The landscape of IPI on the web There are many channels through which attackers might try to send prompt injections. However, one location is particularly easy to observe - the public web. Here, threat actors may simply seed prompt injections on websites in hope of corrupting AI systems that browse them. Public research confirms these attacks ar

enrichment.technical_details: Indirect Prompt Injection (IPI) occurs when an AI system ingests third-party content containing hidden adversarial instructions that the model treats as legitimate commands. Attack vectors include: (1) Concealment in HTML comments, CSS (display:none, 1px font, transparent colors), aria-hidden/visually-hidden attributes, and meta-tag namespace spoofing; (2) Role impersonation and 'system override' tag spoofing; (3) Trigger phrases such as 'Ignore previous instructions,' 'If you are an LLM,' 'Disregard all prior prompts'; (4) Obfuscation via zero-width Unicode, homoglyphs, fragmentation, Base64 encoding, and DOM/runtime assembly; (5) Jailbreak-flavored payloads combined with social engineering. Payload categories observed in the wild range from harmless pranks and SEO manipulation to malicious data exfiltration (API keys, credentials sent to attacker endpoints) and malicious destruction (commands like 'sudo rm -rf').
enrichment.affected_products: Google Gemini (including Gemini in Workspace), GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Anthropic Claude Code, AI-powered CI/CD reviewers, agentic browsers, RAG pipelines, customer-support chatbots, AI-assisted security scanners
enrichment.mitigation: Layered defense approach: (1) Google's AI Vulnerability Reward Program; (2) Continuous red-teaming of Gemini and Workspace AI features; (3) Google's layered defense strategy; (4) Workspace's continuous approach to mitigating indirect prompt injections; (5) Real-time detection and neutralization at Google scale; (6) Defender best practices: enforce strict data-vs-instruction boundaries in agent design, apply 'Spotlighting'/instruction-hierarchy techniques, sanitize/strip hidden HTML/CSS/metadata before LLM ingestion, constrain tool use with human-in-the-loop for destructive actions, block known-bad destinations (Forcepoint IPI URL blocklist, Palo Alto Networks Advanced DNS Security/URL Filtering/Prisma AIRS/Prisma Browser); (7) Adversarial training of models against IPI patterns.

findings.title: Google Workspace’s continuous approach to mitigating indirect prompt injections
findings.summary: Posted by Adam Gavish, Google GenAI Security Team Indirect prompt injection (IPI) is an evolving threat vector targeting users of complex AI applications with multiple data sources, such as Workspace with Gemini. This technique enables the attacker to influence the behavior of an LLM by injecting malicious instructions into the data or tools used by the LLM as it completes the user’s query. This may even be possible without any input directly from the user. IPI is not the kind of technical problem you “solve” and move on. Sophisticated LLMs with increasing use of agentic automation combined with a wide range of content create an ultra-dynamic and evolving playground for adversarial attacks. That’s why Google takes a sophisticated and comprehensive approach to these attacks. We’re continuously improving LLM resistance to IPI attacks and launching AI application capabilities with ever-improving defenses. Staying ahead of the latest indirect prompt injection attacks is critical to our mission of securing Workspace with Gemini. In our previous blog “ Mitigating prompt injection attacks with a layered defense strategy ”, we reviewed the layered architecture of our IPI defenses. In this blog, we’ll share more detail on the continuous approach we take to improve these defenses and to solve for new attacks. New attack discovery By proactively discovering and cataloging new attack vectors through internal and external programs, we can identify vulnerabilities and deploy robust defenses ahead of adversarial activity. Human Red-Teaming Human Red-Teaming uses adversarial simulations to uncover security and safety vulnerabilities. Specialized teams execute attacks based on realistic user profiles to exploit weaknesses, coordinating with product teams to resolve identified issues. Automated Red-Teaming Automated Red-Teaming is done via dynamic, machine-learning-driven frameworks to stress-test environments. By algorithmically generating and iterating on attack payloads, we can mi

enrichment.technical_details: Indirect prompt injection (IPI) is a class of attack in which an adversary embeds malicious instructions into external content (emails, documents, calendar invites, web pages, files in Drive) that a Gemini-powered Workspace feature later retrieves and feeds to the model when fulfilling a user's request. Because Gemini treats that retrieved content as model input, the injected instructions can steer the model's behavior — exfiltrating data, invoking tools, modifying output, or taking agentic actions on the user's behalf — often without any direct input from the user. The core attack surface is the trust boundary between trusted user instructions and untrusted retrieved content, amplified by agentic/tool-using capabilities and the multiple data sources that Workspace aggregates.
enrichment.affected_products: Google Workspace with Gemini (Gmail, Google Docs editors, Google Drive, Google Chat) and the standalone Gemini app
enrichment.patch_available: true - https://blog.google/security/google-workspaces-continuous-approach-to-mitigating-indirect-prompt-injections/ (mitigations are deployed continuously rather than as a single discrete patch)
enrichment.mitigation: Google describes a defense-in-depth posture rather than a single workaround: (1) Deterministic defenses — a centralized Policy Engine enforcing user confirmation, URL sanitization, and tool-chaining policies; (2) ML-based defenses — classifiers retrained on adversarial data synthesized via the 'Simula' platform; (3) LLM-based defenses — system-prompt engineering instructing Gemini to ignore embedded adversarial instructions; (4) Model hardening — improving Gemini's intrinsic ability to recognize and disregard harmful instructions in retrieved data; (5) Proactive discovery — human red-teaming, automated red-teaming, the Google AI Vulnerability Reward Program, and continuous monitoring of publicly disclosed AI attacks. Administrators are advised to keep Gemini features enabled and rely on Google's layered mitigations; no end-user workaround substitutes these defenses.

findings.title: Architecting Security for Agentic Capabilities in Chrome
findings.summary: Posted by Nathan Parker, Chrome security team Chrome has been advancing the web’s security for well over 15 years, and we’re committed to meeting new challenges and opportunities with AI. Billions of people trust Chrome to keep them safe by default, and this is a responsibility we take seriously. Following the recent launch of Gemini in Chrome and the preview of agentic capabilities , we want to share our approach and some new innovations to improve the safety of agentic browsing. The primary new threat facing all agentic browsers is indirect prompt injection . It can appear in malicious sites, third-party content in iframes, or from user-generated content like user reviews, and can cause the agent to take unwanted actions such as initiating financial transactions or exfiltrating sensitive data. Given this open challenge, we are investing in a layered defense that includes both deterministic and probabilistic defenses to make it difficult and costly for attackers to cause harm. Designing safe agentic browsing for Chrome has involved deep collaboration of security experts across Google. We built on Gemini's existing protections and agent security principles and have implemented several new layers for Chrome. We’re introducing a user alignment critic where the agent’s actions are vetted by a separate model that is isolated from untrusted content. We’re also extending Chrome’s origin-isolation capabilities to constrain what origins the agent can interact with, to just those that are relevant to the task. Our layered defense also includes user confirmations for critical steps, real-time detection of threats , and red-teaming and response . We’ll step through these layers below. Checking agent outputs with User Alignment Critic The main planning model for Gemini uses page content shared in Chrome to decide what action to take next. Exposure to untrusted web content means it is inherently vulnerable to indirect prompt injection. We use techniques like spotlighting that di

enrichment.technical_details: The vulnerability class is indirect prompt injection against agentic AI browsers. Attackers plant adversarial instructions in content the agent later consumes (malicious web pages, iframes with third-party content, user-generated content like reviews or comments, shared documents, calendar invites, or emails). When users ask Gemini in Chrome to perform multi-step tasks, the LLM may treat untrusted page content as authoritative instructions and act on them, potentially exfiltrating data via disguised external image/URL requests, navigating to attacker-chosen origins, or performing sensitive actions without user consent. The GeminiJack variant exploits Gemini Enterprise's RAG pipeline where indirect prompt injection in a shared Google Doc, Calendar invite, or email is retrieved and executed silently, exfiltrating Gmail/Docs/Calendar contents via outbound image requests. No CVE was assigned to either the Chrome agentic capability disclosure or GeminiJack.
enrichment.affected_products: Google Chrome with Gemini in Chrome agentic capabilities (Gemini Enterprise and Vertex AI Search also affected by related GeminiJack vulnerability)
enrichment.mitigation: No traditional software patch; Google deployed layered architectural defenses in Chrome and Gemini: (1) User Alignment Critic that vets proposed agent actions against the user's stated goal, (2) Agent Origin Sets restricting the agent's network reach to only task-relevant origins, (3) explicit user confirmation prompts before sensitive actions (banking/medical sites, Password Manager sign-ins, purchases/payments), (4) parallel real-time prompt-injection classifier that can abort or re-prompt the user, (5) 'Spotlighting' markers telling the model to prioritize system/user instructions over untrusted web content, and (6) continuous automated red-teaming against sandboxed malicious sites. Defenders should treat all retrieved web/document content as untrusted, use Gemini Enterprise tenant controls, restrict document sharing, and monitor for anomalous outbound image/URL requests generated by AI assistants.

findings.title: Rust in Android: move fast and fix things
findings.summary: Posted by Jeff Vander Stoep, Android Last year, we wrote about why a memory safety strategy that focuses on vulnerability prevention in new code quickly yields durable and compounding gains. This year we look at how this approach isn’t just fixing things, but helping us move faster . The 2025 data continues to validate the approach, with memory safety vulnerabilities falling below 20% of total vulnerabilities for the first time. Updated data for 2025. This data covers first-party and third-party (open source) code changes to the Android platform across C, C++, Java, Kotlin, and Rust. This post is published a couple of months before the end of 2025, but Android’s industry-standard 90-day patch window means that these results are very likely close to final. We can and will accelerate patching when necessary. We adopted Rust for its security and are seeing a 1000x reduction in memory safety vulnerability density compared to Android’s C and C++ code . But the biggest surprise was Rust's impact on software delivery. With Rust changes having a 4x lower rollback rate and spending 25% less time in code review , the safer path is now also the faster one. In this post, we dig into the data behind this shift and also cover: How we’re expanding our reach: We're pushing to make secure code the default across our entire software stack. We have updates on Rust adoption in first-party apps, the Linux kernel, and firmware. Our first rust memory safety vulnerability...almost: We'll analyze a near-miss memory safety bug in unsafe Rust: how it happened, how it was mitigated, and steps we're taking to prevent recurrence. It’s also a good chance to answer the question “if Rust can have memory safety issues, why bother at all?” Building Better Software, Faster Developing an operating system requires the low-level control and predictability of systems programming languages like C, C++, and Rust. While Java and Kotlin are important for Android platform development, their role is complementa

enrichment.technical_details: CVE-2025-48530 is a Remote Code Execution vulnerability in the Android System component caused by incorrect bounds checks leading to out-of-bounds accesses. The flaw requires no user interaction or privileges, is reachable over the network, and has High impact to Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability. This class of memory-safety defect is exactly the type that Rust adoption in Android is intended to prevent.
enrichment.affected_products: Google Android (Android System component), Android 16 and earlier
enrichment.mitigation: Apply the August 2025 Android Security Bulletin (2025-08-01 security patch level) on all affected Android devices. Ensure devices are kept current with monthly Android security updates.

findings.title: Mitigating prompt injection attacks with a layered defense strategy
findings.summary: Posted by Adam Gavish, Google GenAI Security Team With the rapid adoption of generative AI, a new wave of threats is emerging across the industry with the aim of manipulating the AI systems themselves. One such emerging attack vector is indirect prompt injections. Unlike direct prompt injections, where an attacker directly inputs malicious commands into a prompt, indirect prompt injections involve hidden malicious instructions within external data sources. These may include emails, documents, or calendar invites that instruct AI to exfiltrate user data or execute other rogue actions. As more governments, businesses, and individuals adopt generative AI to get more done, this subtle yet potentially potent attack becomes increasingly pertinent across the industry, demanding immediate attention and robust security measures. At Google, our teams have a longstanding precedent of investing in a defense-in-depth strategy, including robust evaluation, threat analysis, AI security best practices , AI red-teaming , adversarial training, and model hardening for generative AI tools. This approach enables safer adoption of Gemini in Google Workspace and the Gemini app (we refer to both in this blog as “Gemini” for simplicity). Below we describe our prompt injection mitigation product strategy based on extensive research, development, and deployment of improved security mitigations. A layered security approach Google has taken a layered security approach introducing security measures designed for each stage of the prompt lifecycle. From Gemini 2.5 model hardening, to purpose-built machine learning (ML) models detecting malicious instructions, to system-level safeguards, we are meaningfully elevating the difficulty, expense, and complexity faced by an attacker. This approach compels adversaries to resort to methods that are either more easily identified or demand greater resources. Our model training with adversarial data significantly enhanced our defenses against indirect prompt

enrichment.technical_details: Indirect prompt injection is an attack in which untrusted external content consumed by an LLM/agent (e.g., the body of an email in Gmail, text in a Google Doc, a calendar invite description, a file in Google Drive, or a message in Google Chat) embeds hidden or out-of-band instructions — such as HTML white-text, zero-font text, encoded payloads, or instructions inside images/documents. When Gemini in Workspace is asked to summarize, reply to, or act on that content, the model can be steered away from the user's task and induced to perform attacker-chosen actions: exfiltrating data from prior emails or Drive files, following links to attacker-controlled URLs, or surfacing crafted phishing/credential-harvesting content to the user. Unlike direct prompt injection (user-to-model), the attacker controls the data channel, not the prompt. The risk is amplified in agentic settings where Gemini can invoke tools to read additional mail/files or trigger external actions, because instructions in one piece of content can cascade across the user's data.
enrichment.affected_products: Gemini app (consumer), Gemini in Google Workspace: Gmail, Google Docs editors (Docs, Sheets, Slides), Google Drive, and Google Chat. Google has not published specific build or version numbers; affected deployments are identified at the product/surface level (the Gemini integrations within these Workspace apps).
enrichment.cvss_vector: CVSS vector unavailable. Indirect prompt injection is a class-of-vulnerability affecting LLM-integrated products; no individual CVE or CVSS v3 base vector has been published for the Gemini/Gmail/Docs/Drive/Chat issues addressed by Google's layered defenses.
enrichment.exploit_available: true. Multiple public PoCs exist: (1) 0DIN 'Phishing for Gemini' demonstrates indirect prompt injection against Gemini in Gmail/Workspace via hidden white-text email instructions (https://0din.ai/blog/phishing-for-gemini); (2) SafeBreach Labs disclosed a Gemini voice assistant prompt injection exploit (https://www.safebreach.com/blog/gemini-voice-assistant-prompt-injection-exploit/); (3) HiddenLayer reported Gemini for Workspace vulnerability enabling phishing/content manipulation (https://www.hiddenlayer.com/research/new-gemini-for-workspace-vulnerability-enabling-phishing-content-manipulation).
enrichment.patch_available: true. Google's layered mitigations are being progressively rolled out across Gemini in Workspace (Gmail, Docs editors, Drive, Chat) and the Gemini app. There is no traditional software patch/CVE because the protection is a defense-in-depth architecture rather than a single fix. Advisory URL: https://security.googleblog.com/2025/06/mitigating-prompt-injection-attacks.html
enrichment.active_exploitation: true. Indirect prompt injection attacks against LLM agents, including those targeting Gemini, have been observed in the wild. Google's own blog (Jun 13, 2025) notes that the layered protections have been actively mitigating indirect prompt injection attempts against Gemini, indicating real-world attack activity rather than purely theoretical risk. Unit 42 has documented web-based indirect prompt injection attacks observed against AI agents in real-world settings (https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/ai-agent-prompt-injection/).
enrichment.threat_actors: None known. Google's disclosure of this layered defense strategy does not attribute the indirect prompt injection technique to any specific named threat actor group. Separately, Google Threat Intelligence Group has reported that Iranian (APT42), Chinese (APT41), North Korean, and Russian state-aligned actors have abused Gemini for productivity tasks, reconnaissance, and payload development, but those reports do not document those actors exploiting indirect prompt injection against Gemini itself.
enrichment.mitigation: Until model-level robustness fully solves the issue, Google recommends and has rolled out a layered defense strategy for Gemini in Workspace: (1) prompt-injection content classifiers that screen untrusted content for injection attempts; (2) 'security thought reinforcement' via system prompts/model hardening so the model resists being hijacked; (3) markdown sanitization plus suspicious-URL redaction (URLs flagged by Google Safe Browsing are stripped or rendered as plain text); (4) a Human-In-The-Loop (HITL) confirmation framework requiring user approval before sensitive actions; and (5) end-user security notifications that warn users when suspicious or potentially unsafe AI behavior is detected. Administrators can review these protections in Gemini Workspace settings (e.g., 'Workspace smart feature' controls and Gemini classification settings).

findings.title: Countering Chinese State-Sponsored Actors Compromise of Networks Worldwide to Feed Global Espionage System
findings.summary: Executive summary People’s Republic of China (PRC) state-sponsored cyber threat actors are targeting networks globally, including, but not limited to, telecommunications, government, transportation, lodging, and military infrastructure networks. While these actors focus on large backbone routers of major telecommunications providers, as well as provider edge (PE) and customer edge (CE) routers, they also leverage compromised devices and trusted connections to pivot into other networks. These actors often modify routers to maintain persistent, long-term access to networks. This activity partially overlaps with cyber threat actor reporting by the cybersecurity industry—commonly referred to as Salt Typhoon, OPERATOR PANDA, RedMike, UNC5807, and GhostEmperor, among others. The authoring agencies are not adopting a particular commercial naming convention and hereafter refer to those responsible for the cyber threat activity more generically as “Advanced Persistent Threat (APT) actors” throughout this advisory. This cluster of cyber threat activity has been observed in the United States, Australia, Canada, New Zealand, the United Kingdom, and other areas globally. This Cybersecurity Advisory (CSA) includes observations from various government and industry investigations where the APT actors targeted internal enterprise environments, as well as systems and networks that deliver services directly to customers. This CSA details the tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) leveraged by these APT actors to facilitate detection and threat hunting, and provides mitigation guidance to reduce the risk from these APT actors and their TTPs. This CSA is being released by the following authoring and co-sealing agencies: United States National Security Agency (NSA) United States Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) United States Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) United States Department of Defense Cyber Crime Center (DC3) Australian Signals Directorate’s Austra

enrichment.technical_details: PRC state-sponsored actors target backbone routers, provider edge (PE) and customer edge (CE) routers at telecommunications providers. They exploit publicly known CVEs on edge devices (Ivanti Connect/Policy Secure, Palo Alto PAN-OS GlobalProtect, Cisco IOS XE Web UI, Cisco Smart Install), often chaining authentication-bypass vulnerabilities with command-injection flaws. Post-exploitation activities include: (1) using GRE and other tunneling protocols for C2 and data exfiltration; (2) creating virtualized containers (GuestShell) on network devices to evade host-based detection; (3) modifying router configurations (ACLs frequently named 'access-list 20') to maintain persistent long-term access and pivot via trusted connections.
enrichment.affected_products: Ivanti Connect Secure (9.x, 22.x) and Ivanti Policy Secure (9.x, 22.x); Palo Alto Networks PAN-OS GlobalProtect (11.1 prior to 11.1.2-h3, 11.0 prior to 11.0.4-h1, 10.2 prior to 10.2.9-h1); Cisco IOS XE Web Management Interface (all versions with HTTP/HTTPS server enabled); Cisco IOS and IOS XE Smart Install (any unpatched device); suspected targets include Fortinet firewalls, Juniper firewalls, Microsoft Exchange, Nokia routers/switches, Sierra Wireless devices, and SonicWall firewalls
enrichment.cvss_score: 10.0 - Advisory aggregates multiple CVEs (no single advisory score); highest per-CVE scores: CVE-2024-3400 = 10.0, CVE-2023-20198 = 10.0, CVE-2018-0171 = 9.8, CVE-2024-21887 = 9.1, CVE-2023-46805 = 8.2, CVE-2023-20273 = 7.2
enrichment.exploit_available: true - Public PoCs and exploit code are available on GitHub for CVE-2024-3400 (https://github.com/retkoussa/CVE-2024-3400) and CVE-2023-20198 (https://github.com/G4sul1n/Cisco-IOS-XE-CVE-2023-20198), plus Exploit-DB entry 51996
enrichment.patch_available: true - Patches available for all CVEs: Ivanti Connect Secure 9.1R15.3/9.1R16.3/22.1R6.1/22.2R4.1/22.3R1.1/22.4R1.1 and Policy Secure 9.1R18 (CVE-2023-46805/CVE-2024-21887); Palo Alto PAN-OS hotfixes 11.1.2-h3/11.0.4-h1/10.2.9-h1 (CVE-2024-3400); Cisco IOS XE software updates (advisory cisco-sa-iosxe-webui-privesc-j22SaA4z for CVE-2023-20198/CVE-2023-20273); Cisco Smart Install disablement or patched IOS/IOS XE images (CVE-2018-0171)
enrichment.active_exploitation: true - CISA confirms active in-the-wild exploitation by PRC state-sponsored actors worldwide since at least 2021; campaign overlaps with industry-tracked names Salt Typhoon, OPERATOR PANDA, RedMike, UNC5807, and GhostEmperor/VOLT TYPHOON (CISA AA25-239A, Aug 28/Sep 3, 2025; corroborated by Picus Security, SafeBreach, and Forward Networks analyses). Exploitation of zero-day vulnerabilities has not been observed in this campaign.
enrichment.threat_actors: Salt Typhoon, OPERATOR PANDA, RedMike, UNC5807, GhostEmperor (also tracked as VOLT TYPHOON/BRONZE SILHOUETTE); PRC MSS-contracted entities Sichuan Juxinhe Network Technology Co. Ltd., Beijing Huanyu Tianqiong Information Technology Co., Ltd., and Sichuan Zhixin Ruijie Network Technology Co., Ltd.
enrichment.mitigation: 1) Prioritize patching all CVEs identified in CISA's Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog. 2) Regularly review network device logs and configurations for unexpected activity, especially GRE or unauthorized tunneling. 3) Apply Cisco-specific hardening: Embedded Event Manager (EEM) policies to snapshot running configurations and detect/rollback malicious changes; review Cisco IOS/IOS XE hardening and forensic guides. 4) Ensure management-plane VRFs cannot receive traffic from the data/forwarding plane. 5) Upgrade unsupported network devices to vendor-supported replacements. 6) Segment and monitor north-south and east-west traffic at the network edge.

findings.title: Russian GRU Targeting Western Logistics Entities and Technology Companies
findings.summary: Executive Summary This joint cybersecurity advisory (CSA) highlights a Russian state-sponsored cyber campaign targeting Western logistics entities and technology companies. This includes those involved in the coordination, transport, and delivery of foreign assistance to Ukraine. Since 2022, Western logistics entities and IT companies have faced an elevated risk of targeting by the Russian General Staff Main Intelligence Directorate (GRU) 85th Main Special Service Center (85th GTsSS), military unit 26165—tracked in the cybersecurity community under several names (see “ Cybersecurity Industry Tracking ”). The actors’ cyber espionage-oriented campaign, targeting technology companies and logistics entities, uses a mix of previously disclosed tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs). The authoring agencies expect similar targeting and TTP use to continue. Executives and network defenders at logistics entities and technology companies should recognize the elevated threat of unit 26165 targeting, increase monitoring and threat hunting for known TTPs and indicators of compromise (IOCs), and posture network defenses with a presumption of targeting. This cyber espionage-oriented campaign targeting logistics entities and technology companies uses a mix of previously disclosed TTPs and is likely connected to these actors’ wide scale targeting of IP cameras in Ukraine and bordering NATO nations. The following authors and co-sealers are releasing this CSA: United States National Security Agency (NSA) United States Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) United Kingdom National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC-UK) Germany Federal Intelligence Service (BND) Bundesnachrichtendienst Germany Federal Office for Information Security (BSI) Bundesamt für Sicherheit in der Informationstechnik Germany Federal Office for the Protection of the Constitution (BfV) Bundesamt für Verfassungsschutz Czech Republic Military Intelligence (VZ) Vojenské zpravodajství Czech Republic National Cyber and Infor

enrichment.technical_details: CVE-2023-23397 is a critical NTLM credential-leak vulnerability in Microsoft Outlook for Windows. When Outlook processes a specially crafted email containing an extended MAPI property (PidLidReminderFileParameter / ReminderSoundFile) that points to a remote UNC path (typically an SMB server), Outlook automatically initiates an SMB authentication to that attacker-controlled server, leaking the victim's NTLMv2 hash without any user interaction. The leaked hash can be relayed or cracked to enable lateral movement. CVE-2023-38831 in WinRAR allows RCE by spoofing a benign file within a ZIP/RAR archive so that opening the visible 'benign' file triggers execution of a hidden payload. CVE-2020-12641 in Roundcube allows command injection via shell metacharacters in the _im_convert_path configuration. CVE-2020-35730 allows stored XSS in Roundcube, and CVE-2021-44026 allows SQL injection via search/search_params. After exploitation, the actors used living-off-the-land binaries (ntdsutil, wevtutil, vssadmin, ADExplorer, schtasks, wmic, net, reg, ssh) and credential-dumping utilities for lateral movement and data theft.
enrichment.affected_products: Microsoft Outlook for Windows (CVE-2023-23397: Outlook 2013 RT/SP1, Outlook 2016, Office LTSC 2021, Microsoft 365 Apps for Enterprise; Outlook for Android/iOS not affected); RARLAB WinRAR before 6.23 (CVE-2023-38831); Roundcube Webmail before 1.4.4 (CVE-2020-12641); Roundcube Webmail before 1.3.17 and 1.4.x before 1.4.12 (CVE-2021-44026); Roundcube Webmail (CVE-2020-35730)
enrichment.threat_actors: GRU Unit 26165 (85th Main Special Service Center / military unit 26165), tracked by industry as APT28, Fancy Bear, Strontium, Sofacy, Sednit, Forest Blizzard, IRON TWILIGHT, and BlueDelta
enrichment.mitigation: Apply March 14, 2023 Microsoft security updates for CVE-2023-23397 to all Outlook for Windows instances and reboot. Upgrade WinRAR to 6.23 or later. Update Roundcube Webmail to 1.4.4 or later (CVE-2020-12641) and to 1.3.17 / 1.4.12 or later (CVE-2021-44026). Enforce phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2/WebAuthn, PIV smartcards) on all email and VPN accounts and require periodic re-authentication. Block outbound SMB (TCP/445) and NetBIOS traffic from user and server segments; disable NTLM where possible and migrate to PKI-based authentication. Separate privileged accounts by role (e.g., distinct email-administrator and domain-administrator accounts) and audit them regularly. Remove credentials stored in Group Policy Preferences (GPP) and reset affected passwords. Use account-throttling instead of account-lockout and alert on anomalous attempts. Block or alert on logins from public VPN/Tor exit-node IP ranges. Disable UPnP, P2P, and anonymous-visitor features on IP cameras and edge routers; patch firmware; replace end-of-life devices; front cameras with firewalls/VPNs and restrict to authenticated RTSP. Audit email-security settings (SPF/DKIM/DMARC) and review Exchange Online transport rules to block IOCs. Deploy EDR/NGAV on mail servers and domain controllers; enable Windows Attack Surface Reduction rules to block executable content from email and user-writable directories. Use Sigma rules to detect suspicious wmic, net, vssadmin, and wevtutil usage. Hunt for IOCs (IPs, domains, file hashes, YARA rules) provided in the advisory appendix.

findings.title: Browser Security: Zero-Days Are Only Part of the Problem

enrichment.technical_details: The piece is a thought-leadership/threat-intelligence blog post, not a vulnerability disclosure. It does not describe a specific CVE. Technical content covers general browser-based attack vectors (rendering-logic exploitation, JavaScript execution, document-handling issues, memory-safety weaknesses, sandbox escape/privilege escalation), the chained nature of browser-to-system compromise, and a defensive technique called JavaScript Language Randomization (JSLR), a moving-target defense inside Falcon Secure Access that continuously randomizes the JavaScript runtime to impede exploitation of browser vulnerabilities. Cited macro statistics: Verizon 2026 DBIR (vulnerability exploitation surpassed stolen credentials as the top breach entry point in 2025) and the CrowdStrike 2026 Global Threat Report (42% of vulnerabilities were exploited before public disclosure).
enrichment.affected_products: Affected products unavailable. The article does not identify specific affected software versions; it discusses browser-class attack surface broadly (e.g., Chromium-based browsers, rendering, JavaScript, document handling, memory-safety weaknesses) and references CrowdStrike Falcon Secure Access as a defensive product.
enrichment.exploit_available: Exploit availability unknown. The blog does not reference a specific PoC or weaponized exploit for any named vulnerability; it discusses general exploit categories (phishing, session hijacking, malicious downloads, clickjacking, cross-site scripting, HTML smuggling, credential theft).
enrichment.patch_available: Patch availability unknown. The article is not a vulnerability advisory for a specific flaw, so no vendor patch for a specific CVE is referenced. CrowdStrike Falcon Secure Access is positioned as a defensive product rather than a patch for a disclosed vulnerability.
enrichment.active_exploitation: False. The blog does not report confirmed in-the-wild exploitation of any specific, named vulnerability; it cites the CrowdStrike 2026 Global Threat Report statistic that 42% of vulnerabilities were exploited before public disclosure as an industry trend, without attributing exploitation to a specific CVE.
enrichment.mitigation: No vendor patch for a specific vulnerability is provided. Recommended hardening: (1) adopt browser-level in-session defenses that block phishing, adversary-in-the-middle, session-hijacking/MFA-bypass, credential theft, and data exfiltration at execution; (2) use moving-target / runtime-randomization techniques such as JavaScript Language Randomization (JSLR) in CrowdStrike Falcon Secure Access to harden the JavaScript runtime against exploitation; (3) reduce reliance on patch timing alone by adding protections that work before patches are deployed; (4) extend coverage to unmanaged/contractor/third-party devices that traditional endpoint agents miss.

findings.title: 94% of Organizations Report Cloud Breaches: CrowdStrike State of CDR Survey

enrichment.technical_details: Technical details unavailable — this is not a vulnerability advisory. The referenced content is the 'CrowdStrike State of CDR Survey: Highlights & Key Takeaways' (published June 22, 2026). It summarizes survey findings on cloud detection-and-response maturity, reporting that 94% of organizations experienced cloud intrusions resulting in data exposure or exfiltration, 73% cannot consistently guarantee cloud intrusion detection, 78% cannot reliably distinguish legitimate activity from malicious behavior, 68% take 15+ minutes to detect cloud intrusions (51% take at least an hour), 91% cannot contain cloud intrusions in real time, and 81% of organizations running AI/ML workloads in the cloud have experienced incidents targeting those environments.
enrichment.affected_products: Not applicable. The referenced item is a survey/research report and does not identify any specific CrowdStrike or third-party software/hardware products or versions as vulnerable.
enrichment.cvss_score: CVSS score unavailable. The referenced item is a survey report and has no associated CVE or CVSS score.
enrichment.cvss_vector: CVSS vector unavailable. The referenced item is a survey report and has no associated CVE or CVSS score.
enrichment.active_exploitation: false. The referenced item is a CrowdStrike industry survey on cloud detection-and-response trends. It is not a vulnerability advisory and does not report active exploitation of a specific CVE.
enrichment.threat_actors: None known. The referenced item is a CrowdStrike industry survey on cloud detection and response (CDR) trends; it does not identify specific threat actor groups, APT campaigns, or ransomware operators.
enrichment.mitigation: Mitigation steps unavailable (no patch is applicable because this is not a vulnerability). The survey's published recommendations for improving cloud detection-and-response posture are: (1) achieve complete visibility across the cloud control plane and workloads; (2) reduce tool fragmentation by integrating cloud detections into SOC/SIEM workflows; and (3) move away from heavy reliance on manual investigations to improve response speed. CrowdStrike also points readers to its 'State of CDR Survey' report and 'Cloud Detection and Response Survival Guide for the SOC' as supporting resources.

findings.title: CrowdStrike Uncovers New Prompt Injection Techniques
Bringing Rust to the Pixel Baseband
findings.summary: Posted by Jiacheng Lu, Software Engineer, Google Pixel Team Google is continuously advancing the security of Pixel devices. We have been focusing on hardening the cellular baseband modem against exploitation. Recognizing the risks associated within the complex modem firmware, Pixel 9 shipped with mitigations against a range of memory-safety vulnerabilities. For Pixel 10, Google is advancing its proactive security measures further. Following our previous discussion on "Deploying Rust in Existing Firmware Codebases" , this post shares a concrete application: integrating a memory-safe Rust DNS(Domain Name System) parser into the modem firmware. The new Rust-based DNS parser significantly reduces our security risk by mitigating an entire class of vulnerabilities in a risky area, while also laying the foundation for broader adoption of memory-safe code in other areas. Here we share our experience of working on it, and hope it can inspire the use of more memory safe languages in low-level environments. Why Modem Memory Safety Can’t Wait In recent years, we have seen increasing interest in the cellular modem from attackers and security researchers. For example, Google's Project Zero gained remote code execution on Pixel modems over the Internet. Pixel modem has tens of Megabytes of executable code. Given the complexity and remote attack surface of the modem, other critical memory safety vulnerabilities may remain in the predominantly memory-unsafe firmware code. Why DNS? The DNS protocol is most commonly known in the context of browsers finding websites. With the evolution of cellular technology, modern cellular communications have migrated to digital data networks; consequently, even basic operations such as call forwarding rely on DNS services. DNS is a complex protocol and requires parsing of untrusted data, which can lead to vulnerabilities, particularly when implemented in a memory-unsafe language (example: CVE-2024-27227 ). Implementing the DNS parser in Rust offers
